entries.name: Fed Interest Rate Decision
entries.definition: The Federal Reserve's Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) sets the target range for the federal funds rate — the overnight rate banks charge each other. This is the most important interest rate in global finance.
entries.whyItMatters: It directly affects mortgage rates, business borrowing, the dollar's strength, and the discount rate used to value every stock on Earth. A surprise hike or cut can move every market within seconds.

marketReaction.higher: Surprise hike: USD strengthens, stocks fall (especially growth/tech), bond yields rise, gold typically dips.
marketReaction.lower: Surprise cut or dovish hold: USD weakens, stocks rally, bond prices rise, gold strengthens.

entries.name: FOMC Press Conference
entries.definition: 30 minutes after the rate decision, the Fed Chair takes questions from reporters. The tone — hawkish (favoring tighter policy) or dovish (favoring easier policy) — often moves markets more than the rate decision itself.
entries.whyItMatters: Markets parse every word for hints about the next meeting. A single phrase ('we are not even thinking about thinking about raising rates') can re-rate trillions of dollars in assets.

marketReaction.higher: Hawkish tone (signals more hikes): USD up, stocks down, yields rise.
marketReaction.lower: Dovish tone (signals cuts or pause): USD down, stocks rally, yields fall.

entries.name: FOMC Meeting Minutes
entries.definition: Detailed summary of the discussion at the most recent FOMC meeting. Reveals the range of views among the 12 voting members and any dissent.
entries.whyItMatters: Sometimes contradicts the public statement and shows where the committee is split. Surprises about hawkish or dovish dissent can shift rate-cut expectations.

marketReaction.higher: Hawkish minutes (members worried about inflation): USD up, stocks dip, yields rise.
marketReaction.lower: Dovish minutes (members worried about growth): USD down, stocks rally, yields fall.

entries.name: ECB Interest Rate Decision
entries.definition: The European Central Bank sets the deposit rate, main refinancing rate, and marginal lending facility rate for the 20-country Eurozone.
entries.whyItMatters: Sets monetary policy for the world's second-largest currency bloc. Drives EUR/USD, European bond yields, and the Euro Stoxx index.

marketReaction.higher: Hawkish surprise: EUR strengthens, European stocks fall, bund yields rise.
marketReaction.lower: Dovish surprise: EUR weakens, European stocks rally, bund yields fall.

entries.name: BoE Interest Rate Decision
entries.definition: The Bank of England's Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) sets the Bank Rate. Voting splits among the 9 members are watched closely.
entries.whyItMatters: Drives GBP and UK bond yields. The MPC vote breakdown often signals the next move — a 5-4 vote vs. unanimous matters.

marketReaction.higher: Hawkish: GBP strengthens, UK stocks dip, gilt yields rise.
marketReaction.lower: Dovish: GBP weakens, UK stocks rally, gilt yields fall.

entries.name: BoJ Interest Rate Decision
entries.definition: The Bank of Japan sets short-term policy rates and has used yield curve control on 10-year JGB yields. Japan's policy is unique because rates were negative or zero for years.
entries.whyItMatters: Any shift away from ultra-loose policy moves the yen sharply and ripples through global carry trades.

marketReaction.higher: Hawkish surprise (rare): JPY strengthens sharply, Nikkei falls, JGB yields rise.
marketReaction.lower: Continued dovish stance: JPY weakens, Nikkei rallies.

entries.name: Non-Farm Payrolls
entries.frequency: Monthly, first Friday at 8:30 AM ET
entries.definition: The monthly count of jobs added or lost in the US economy, excluding farm workers, government employees, private household staff, and non-profits.
entries.whyItMatters: The most-watched US labor market report. The Fed uses it to gauge economic strength and decide on rates. Massive volatility in the 5 minutes after the release.

marketReaction.higher: Stronger than expected: USD typically strengthens (Fed may stay hawkish), stocks reaction depends on context — strong economy is good but rate-hike fears can dominate.
marketReaction.lower: Weaker than expected: USD weakens (Fed may cut), stocks often rally on rate-cut hopes, gold strengthens.

entries.name: Unemployment Rate
entries.definition: Percentage of the labor force actively seeking work. Released alongside Non-Farm Payrolls.
entries.whyItMatters: Half of the Fed's dual mandate is 'maximum employment.' The Fed eyes 3.5–4.5% as roughly full employment in the US.

marketReaction.higher: Higher unemployment: USD weakens, stocks may rally on rate-cut hopes, bond yields fall.
marketReaction.lower: Lower unemployment: USD strengthens (tight labor market = inflationary), bond yields rise.

entries.name: Initial Jobless Claims
entries.definition: Number of Americans filing for unemployment benefits for the first time in the past week.
entries.whyItMatters: The most timely labor market indicator — only one week stale. A sustained rise above ~250K signals labor market weakness.

marketReaction.higher: Higher claims: USD weakens slightly, stocks may rally on rate-cut hopes.
marketReaction.lower: Lower claims: USD strengthens slightly, signals tight labor market.

entries.name: Average Hourly Earnings
entries.definition: Year-over-year change in average hourly wages for private-sector workers. A leading indicator of consumer inflation.
entries.whyItMatters: Wage growth feeds directly into services inflation. The Fed watches this closely — wages rising faster than productivity is a red flag.

marketReaction.higher: Stronger wages: USD up (Fed stays hawkish), stocks down (rate fears), bond yields rise.
marketReaction.lower: Weaker wages: USD down, stocks rally, bond yields fall.

entries.name: CPI YoY
entries.definition: Year-over-year change in the Consumer Price Index — the cost of a basket of goods and services bought by typical urban households.
entries.whyItMatters: Headline inflation. The Fed targets 2% (using PCE, but CPI moves markets more). Above 3% = restrictive policy; below 2% = stimulus likely.

marketReaction.higher: Hot CPI: USD strengthens (Fed hawkish), stocks fall, bond yields rise, gold mixed.
marketReaction.lower: Cool CPI: USD weakens (Fed dovish), stocks rally, bond yields fall.

entries.name: Core CPI YoY
entries.definition: CPI excluding volatile food and energy prices. The Fed watches Core more than headline because it shows the underlying trend.
entries.whyItMatters: Removes noise from oil shocks and weather. If Core stays sticky while headline falls, the Fed stays hawkish.

marketReaction.higher: Sticky Core CPI: USD up, stocks down, yields up. More important than headline for Fed pricing.
marketReaction.lower: Cooling Core CPI: USD down, stocks up, yields down.

entries.name: Core PCE Price Index YoY
entries.frequency: Monthly, last week of the month
entries.definition: The Fed's preferred inflation gauge — Personal Consumption Expenditures index excluding food and energy.
entries.whyItMatters: This is THE number the Fed targets at 2%. More accurate than CPI for actual consumer behavior because it accounts for substitution.

marketReaction.higher: Hot Core PCE: USD up, stocks down, yields up. Confirms Fed hawkish stance.
marketReaction.lower: Cool Core PCE: USD down, stocks rally, yields down. Validates rate-cut hopes.

entries.name: Producer Price Index
entries.definition: Wholesale prices charged by producers — a leading indicator of consumer inflation 1-3 months ahead.
entries.whyItMatters: If producer prices are rising fast, those costs eventually pass to consumers. Markets use it to handicap the next CPI.

marketReaction.higher: Hot PPI: previews hot CPI — USD up, stocks down, yields up.
marketReaction.lower: Cool PPI: previews cool CPI — USD down, stocks up, yields down.

entries.name: GDP QoQ
entries.definition: Quarter-over-quarter change in Gross Domestic Product, annualized. The broadest measure of economic activity.
entries.whyItMatters: Defines whether the economy is in recession (two consecutive negative quarters) or expanding. Less market-moving than higher-frequency data because it's released months after the quarter ends.

marketReaction.higher: Strong GDP: USD up, stocks may rally on growth, bond yields rise.
marketReaction.lower: Weak GDP: USD down, stocks may fall on recession fears OR rally on rate-cut hopes — depends on context.

entries.name: Retail Sales MoM
entries.definition: Month-over-month change in total sales at retail and food service establishments. Measures consumer spending — ~70% of US GDP.
entries.whyItMatters: Consumer spending is the engine of the US economy. Strong retail sales = strong economy = Fed stays hawkish.

marketReaction.higher: Strong retail sales: USD up, stocks may rally on growth, bond yields rise.
marketReaction.lower: Weak retail sales: USD down, stocks fall on recession fears, bond yields fall.

entries.name: ISM Manufacturing PMI
entries.definition: Survey of US manufacturing executives. A reading above 50 indicates expansion; below 50 indicates contraction.
entries.whyItMatters: Leading indicator of manufacturing health. Sustained readings below 48 historically precede recessions.

marketReaction.higher: Above 50 expanding: USD up, stocks rally on growth, yields rise.
marketReaction.lower: Below 50 contracting: USD down, stocks may fall, yields fall on rate-cut hopes.

entries.name: ISM Services PMI
entries.definition: Survey of US services-sector executives. Services are ~80% of the US economy, so this matters more than manufacturing PMI.
entries.whyItMatters: Services are stickier than manufacturing — strong services PMI means inflation pressure persists.

marketReaction.higher: Strong services PMI: USD up, stocks rally on growth, bond yields rise.
marketReaction.lower: Weak services PMI: USD down, stocks fall, yields fall.

entries.name: Consumer Confidence
entries.definition: Survey by the Conference Board measuring how consumers feel about current and future economic conditions.
entries.whyItMatters: Confident consumers spend more. A drop signals weaker future retail sales and growth.

marketReaction.higher: Higher confidence: USD up modestly, stocks rally on growth optimism.
marketReaction.lower: Lower confidence: USD down modestly, stocks may fall on recession fears.

entries.name: Michigan Consumer Sentiment
entries.definition: University of Michigan survey of consumer attitudes. Includes 1-year and 5-year inflation expectations — the latter is closely watched by the Fed.
entries.whyItMatters: The 5-year inflation expectation embedded here is one of the Fed's favorite anchors. If it un-anchors above 3%, the Fed gets hawkish.

marketReaction.higher: Higher inflation expectations: USD up, stocks down, bond yields rise.
marketReaction.lower: Lower inflation expectations: USD down, stocks rally, yields fall.

entries.name: Building Permits
entries.definition: Number of new residential construction permits issued. A leading indicator of housing starts.
entries.whyItMatters: Permits today become construction in 1-3 months. Tracks the housing market's trajectory.

marketReaction.higher: More permits: housing strong, USD up modestly, homebuilder stocks rally.
marketReaction.lower: Fewer permits: housing weak, homebuilder stocks fall.

entries.name: Existing Home Sales
entries.definition: Annualized rate of existing-home sale closings (completed transactions, not pending).
entries.whyItMatters: Largest part of the US housing market. Reflects mortgage-rate sensitivity — rises when rates fall.

marketReaction.higher: Strong sales: housing recovering, homebuilder stocks rally.
marketReaction.lower: Weak sales: housing slumping, homebuilder stocks fall.

entries.name: Trade Balance
entries.definition: Difference between exports and imports. The US runs a persistent trade deficit (imports > exports).
entries.whyItMatters: Tracks competitiveness and demand for the dollar. A widening deficit can pressure USD long-term.

marketReaction.higher: Smaller deficit / larger surplus: USD up modestly, stocks neutral.
marketReaction.lower: Larger deficit: USD down modestly, stocks neutral.

entries.name: Durable Goods Orders
entries.definition: New orders for items expected to last 3+ years (cars, appliances, machinery, aircraft). Often quoted ex-transportation to remove aircraft volatility.
entries.whyItMatters: Leading indicator of business investment. Rising orders = expansion; falling = recession risk.

marketReaction.higher: Strong durables: USD up, stocks rally on growth.
marketReaction.lower: Weak durables: USD down, stocks may fall, recession concerns rise.

entries.name: Manufacturing PMI Flash
entries.definition: S&P Global's preliminary manufacturing survey for major economies (US, EU, UK, Japan). Released ~10 days before final.
entries.whyItMatters: First glimpse of manufacturing trends each month. 50 separates expansion from contraction.

marketReaction.higher: Above 50 expanding: local currency up, local stocks rally.
marketReaction.lower: Below 50 contracting: local currency down, local stocks may fall.

entries.name: Composite PMI Flash
entries.definition: Combined manufacturing + services PMI, weighted by sector size. Best single read on overall economic momentum.
entries.whyItMatters: Single number summarizing the whole economy's trajectory. Sustained readings below 48 signal recession risk.

marketReaction.higher: Above 50: local currency up, stocks rally.
marketReaction.lower: Below 50: local currency down, stocks fall, rate-cut hopes rise.

entries.name: ZEW Economic Sentiment
entries.definition: Survey of ~350 financial analysts about Germany's expected economic conditions over the next 6 months.
entries.whyItMatters: Germany is the Eurozone's largest economy. ZEW is a leading indicator of Eurozone GDP and a key driver of EUR.

marketReaction.higher: Higher ZEW: EUR strengthens, DAX rallies.
marketReaction.lower: Lower ZEW: EUR weakens, DAX falls.

entries.name: Ifo Business Climate
entries.definition: Survey of ~9000 German firms about current conditions and expectations for the next 6 months.
entries.whyItMatters: Tracks the actual business pulse of Germany — broader than ZEW which surveys analysts. Strong predictor of German GDP.

marketReaction.higher: Stronger Ifo: EUR strengthens, DAX rallies.
marketReaction.lower: Weaker Ifo: EUR weakens, DAX falls.

entries.name: China Manufacturing PMI
entries.frequency: Monthly, end of month (official) and start of month (Caixin)
entries.definition: Two versions: official NBS PMI (large state-owned firms) and Caixin PMI (smaller private firms). Both release the same metric: 50 = expansion threshold.
entries.whyItMatters: China is the world's factory and largest commodities consumer. Strong China PMI lifts copper, oil, iron ore, and AUD/NZD.

marketReaction.higher: Above 50: AUD/NZD strengthen, copper/oil rally, China-exposed stocks rally.
marketReaction.lower: Below 50: AUD/NZD weaken, commodities fall, recession fears.

entries.name: RBA Interest Rate Decision
entries.frequency: 8 times per year (first Tuesday of most months)
entries.definition: Reserve Bank of Australia sets the cash rate. Australia is highly sensitive to commodity prices and China demand.
entries.whyItMatters: Drives AUD and Australian bond yields. AUD is also a proxy for risk sentiment and China exposure.

marketReaction.higher: Hawkish: AUD up, ASX falls, AGB yields rise.
marketReaction.lower: Dovish: AUD down, ASX rallies, yields fall.
